title: Function Calling at the Inference Layer
summary: ChatCompletionsClient supports tools and tool choice. Tool definitions describe caller-defined functions. The model may return message.tool calls.
analogy: The model is a dispatcher, not a mechanic. It writes a work order: call get_weather with city Seattle. Your code is the mechanic that performs the work and returns the result ticket.
diagram:
sequenceDiagram
  participant App as Python app
  participant Model as Chat model
  participant Func as Local function
  App->>Model: messages plus tool definitions
  Model-->>App: assistant message with tool calls
  App->>Func: execute validated function
  Func-->>App: tool result JSON
  App->>Model: prior assistant tool call plus tool message
  Model-->>App: final answer
body:
## First principles

The model cannot actually execute your Python function. It can only request a function call by returning structured arguments. Your code decides whether to run the function, runs it, then sends the result back as a tool message.

## Definition

ChatCompletionsClient supports tools and tool_choice. Tool definitions describe caller-defined functions. The model may return message.tool_calls. The caller executes the requested function and sends ToolMessage results back in a second complete call. This is the raw inference mechanic; MCP standardizes how tools are exposed, but is a separate lane.

## How it fits together

Tool calling is a two-step loop: ask with tool definitions, inspect tool_calls, execute only allowed functions, then continue with assistant message plus ToolMessage. Validate JSON arguments because they came from the model.

## Worked example

```python
import json
import os
from azure.identity import DefaultAzureCredential
from azure.ai.inference import ChatCompletionsClient
from azure.ai.inference.models import (
    AssistantMessage,
    ChatCompletionsToolDefinition,
    FunctionDefinition,
    SystemMessage,
    ToolMessage,
    UserMessage,
)

client = ChatCompletionsClient(
    endpoint=os.environ["AZURE_AI_CHAT_ENDPOINT"],
    credential=DefaultAzureCredential(),
)

def get_support_hours(product: str) -> dict:
    allowed = {"foundry sdk": "Monday to Friday, 9 AM to 5 PM Pacific"}
    return {"product": product, "hours": allowed.get(product.lower(), "Unknown")}

tools = [
    ChatCompletionsToolDefinition(
        function=FunctionDefinition(
            name="get_support_hours",
            description="Return support hours for a product.",
            parameters={
                "type": "object",
                "properties": {"product": {"type": "string"}},
                "required": ["product"],
            },
        )
    )
]

messages = [
    SystemMessage("Use tools when needed. Do not invent support hours."),
    UserMessage("What are the support hours for Foundry SDK?"),
]

first = client.complete(
    model=os.getenv("AZURE_AI_MODEL_DEPLOYMENT_NAME"),
    messages=messages,
    tools=tools,
)
assistant = first.choices[0].message
messages.append(AssistantMessage(content=assistant.content, tool_calls=assistant.tool_calls))

for call in assistant.tool_calls or []:
    if call.function.name != "get_support_hours":
        raise ValueError(f"Unexpected tool: {call.function.name}")
    args = json.loads(call.function.arguments)
    result = get_support_hours(product=args["product"])
    messages.append(ToolMessage(content=json.dumps(result), tool_call_id=call.id))

final = client.complete(
    model=os.getenv("AZURE_AI_MODEL_DEPLOYMENT_NAME"),
    messages=messages,
)
print(final.choices[0].message.content)
```

*Minimal two-call function-calling loop with validation.*

drills.q: Does the model run your function directly?
drills.a: No. It returns a requested tool call; your code executes it.

drills.q: What message sends tool output back?
How is this related to MCP?
drills.a: It is the lower-level mechanism MCP can build on or expose through a standard protocol.

teachItBack: Function calling is a controlled handshake. The model requests, your code validates and executes, then the model summarizes the result. Never execute arbitrary tool names or unvalidated arguments.

glossary.definition: Model-requested function invocation with name and arguments.
Schema describing a callable function to the model.
Setting controlling whether and which tool the model may call.

sources.title: Azure SDK Python tool calling sample
